vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters from Same Character Class
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's maxclassrepeat parameter controls requirements for consecutive repeating characters from the same character class. When set to a positive number, it will reject passwords which contain more than that number of consecutive characters from the same character class. Modify the maxclassrepeat setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to equal to prevent a run of ( + 1) or more identical characters.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat='' if grep -sq maxclassrepeat /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/maxclassrepeat/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^maxclassrepeat") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'settin

triage_decision.reason: The change modifies /etc/security/pwquality.conf to set maxclassrepeat, which enforces a password composition rule. This only affects password creation/change operations and does not disrupt existing authentication sessions or services.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Misconfiguration could prevent users from setting new passwords if the value is too restrictive; May conflict with organizational password policies if not coordinated

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to produce the remediation steps for the given finding: Ensure PAM enforces password requirements - Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters from Same Character Class. The rule is accounts_password_pam_maxclassrepeat. Finding ID same. Severity 2.

We need to modify /etc/security/pwquality.conf to set maxclassrepeat appropriately to prevent a run of (+1) or more identical characters.

The recommendation includes a remediation script: basically set var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat='' if grep -sq maxclassrepeat /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/maxclassrepeat/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Then strip search characters etc. But we need to produce a clear remediation: set maxclassrepeat to a value (like 3?) Actually typical default is maybe 3? The finding wants to enforce a rule; we need to set maxclassrepeat to some value (maybe 3). The description: "Modify the maxclassrepeat setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to equal to prevent a run of ( + 1) or more identical characters." Means if we want to prevent 2 or more identical consecutive characters? Actually "prevent a run of (+1) or more identical characters" means we want to reject passwords with more than X consecutive same characters; setting maxclassrepeat = X will reject passwords with > X consecutive same chars. So to prevent a run of 2 or more identical characters, set maxclassrepeat = 1? Wait: If maxclassrepeat = N, then passwords containing more than N consecutive identical characters are rejected. So to prevent a run of 2 or more identical characters, we set maxclassrepeat = 1 (reject >1 consecutive identical => reject any password with 2 or more same chars). But typical is to limit to 3? Actually many policies set maxclassrepeat = 3 to reject 4 or more same consecutive characters. The description says "prevent a run of ( + 1) or more identical characters." That seems ambiguous. Likely they want to set maxclassrepeat = 3 to prevent 4 or more. But we can follow the recommendation: The remediation script suggests: if grep -sq maxclassrepeat /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/maxclassrepeat/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi . That is to delete any existing maxclassrepeat lines then add a new line? Actually they didn't show adding. Possibly they intend to set it after removing.

We need to output the remediation steps as plain text, likely with commands.

Thus produce:

1. Check current value: grep -i maxclassrepeat /etc/security/pwquality.conf
2. If present, note value.
3. To enforce, set maxclassrepeat = 3 (or maybe 2). We'll decide to set to 3 as common.
4. Use sed to replace or add line.

We'll provide a remediation script:

```
# Determine desired maxclassrepeat value (e.g., 3)
DESIRED=3
# Backup original file
cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak
# Remove any existing maxclassrepeat lines
sed -i '/^\s*maxclassrepeat\s*=/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# Ensure the line is added under the [default] section or at end
if ! grep -q '^\s*\[\s*default\s*\]' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; then
    sed -i '$a\[default\]' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
fi
# Append or set maxclassrepeat line
sed -i "/^\[default\]/a maxclassrepeat = $DESIRED" /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# Verify
grep maxclassrepeat /etc/security/pwquality.conf
```

We need to read file via read_file, but we can also just use run_cmd to modify.

review_feedback: The remediation plan is incomplete and incorrect. The variable var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat is set to an empty string, which would result in maxclassrepeat being set to no value (or default). The plan also only searches /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf instead of the main /etc/security/pwquality.conf file, and the script is truncated, missing the actual assignment of a meaningful value (e.g., 3) to enforce the maximum consecutive repeating characters from the same character class.

The plan also only searches /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf instead of the main /etc/security/pwquality.conf file, and the script is truncated, missing the actual assignment of a meaningful value (e.g., 3) to enforce the maximum consecutive repeating characters from the same character class.
concerns: Variable var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat not assigned a valid integer value.
Script is truncated; missing logic to write the correct setting to the configuration file.
Plan targets files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf rather than the main /etc/security/pwquality.conf as specified in the vulnerability description.
suggested_improvements: Assign var_password_pam_maxclassrepeat an appropriate integer (e.g., 3) based on organizational policy.
Ensure the remediation edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf directly (or includes the .conf.d directory if that is where the setting is managed).
Complete the script: after stripping the key, use sed or echo to set 'maxclassrepeat = <value>' in the config file, handling both replacement and addition cases.
Add a final verification step (e.g., grep) to confirm the setting is present with the expected value.
